name: Calcariidae
description: Longspurs and snow buntings are a family of passerine birds adapted to cold, open habitats in the Northern Hemisphere. They are ground-dwelling birds that breed in the Arctic tundra and alpine meadows and migrate south for winter.

children.name: Plectrophenax
children.description: A genus of largely white buntings that breed in high Arctic regions and winter in temperate zones.

children.name: Plectrophenax nivalis
children.description: The Snow Bunting is a striking white bird with black wing tips in breeding males. It breeds on Arctic tundra across the Northern Hemisphere and winters in open fields and coastal areas further south.

children.name: Plectrophenax hyperboreus
children.description: McKay's Bunting is almost entirely white with limited black wing tips, resembling a pure white Snow Bunting. It is endemic to the Bering Sea islands and winters along the coast of Alaska.

children.name: Calcarius
children.description: A genus of sparrow-like birds with elongated hind claws, found in open habitats of the Northern Hemisphere.

children.name: Calcarius lapponicus
children.description: The Lapland Longspur is a sparrow-sized bird with a black face and chestnut nape in breeding males. It breeds on Arctic tundra circumpolarly and winters in grasslands across temperate regions.

children.name: Calcarius ornatus
children.description: The Chestnut-collared Longspur has a black belly and chestnut nape in breeding males. It breeds in shortgrass prairies of the north-central United States and southern Canada.

children.name: Calcarius mccownii
children.description: A sparrow-like bird with a black bib and chestnut nape in breeding males, found in shortgrass prairies of the north-central United States and southern Canada. It is named after the American ornithologist John McCown and was formerly placed in its own genus Rhynchophanes.

children.name: Calcarius pictus
children.description: A distinctive longspur with a white belly and chestnut wings in breeding males, found in the Arctic tundra of Canada and Alaska. Named after the Scottish naturalist Abraham Smith, it winters in the grasslands of the south-central United States.

children.name: Calcarius bicolor
children.description: A large, robust longspur with a thick, conical bill, a black-and-white head pattern, and chestnut nape in breeding males, found on the Arctic tundra of Canada and wintering in the central grasslands of the United States. It is distinguished from other longspurs by its heavier bill and more deliberate movements.
